reviews.review_text:
I really want to recommend this game but it has one fundamental issue: the object of the game is not designed as the ultimate aim. The object, to raise a family dynasty to take over your village, takes too long to come to fruition. All obstacles are essentially cleared within a few years and all that is left is to wait for your child to age. There is no late game content to keep interest alive. All that is left is to decorate your village, which by the time you can afford to unlock all decorations you're essentially playing a sandbox with no challenges or incentives. Further, the family dynamic is extremely muted - there is no real development except gifting once a season, no real investment in what they're doing or who they are, even the most significant events of marriage and childbirth are merely a generic cutscene.

The game should really be called "Medieval Decorator."

If you're a player who's prepared to grind it out and put the work in, this game is for you.  Personally, I tried twice to get into it and probably will again, but it feels too grindy at times and I lost momentum, and that's with decreased crafting and building times and  sharing the workload on co-op.  I'm not sure what the thinking was behind such extended crafting and building times when the effort required to get ingredients is already strenuous.  There doesn't appear to be any reason to talk to most of the NPC's, the dialogue is boring and loops in circles with no payoff, unless it's quest related.

I played the solo mode but not story mode.
This is a third person city builder with quests (some funny shock humour from npcs).
Being able to customize your game is very helpful, and can add or take away from the difficulty.
I enjoyed being able to extend the seasons to 30 days instead of 3. Playing as a woman or man with a few custom appearances.
Meeting someone, getting to know them, and marrying to extend your legacy (being able to choose sex at birth) is a nice touch too.
Managing work load of civillians is a bit difficult when you get to the "city" stage (I also extended the amount of buildings I could have, so the population was large as well).  When civillians start having kids it's enjoyable to see them waddling around.
It's only crashed on me a couple of times and all I had to do was get rid of some extensive city lights and spread them out a bit.
Overall this is one of my top 10 favourite pc games that can be easilly paused and put down if I get distracted at home.

Cant say Id be ok with a dev team letting someone lie Morri be their voice. The level of disrespect to basic questions and critique is insane. I love this game, and while it has its issues its still a good time. But Morri just insults and is rude in the forums to anyone not agreeing with them. Cant support a dev team thats ok with people like this.

Good fun, both alone and in co-op! Came as a surprise because I'm usually not too big on survival games. It probably helps that it's more than that: Growing your settlement, you have to manage its increasingly complex economy and the workers you recruit. With the game's progression, you should have enough time to figure out the mechanics without being overwhelmed.

One could argue that progression is too slow and there's lots of grinding to be done but tbh this isn't a game you should rush. However, should you wish to do so anyway, there's settings to make your life a little bit easier (infinite stamina or carrying capacity, weaker enemies, etc).

There's certainly lots to do: Building, hunting, crafting, stealing, eating, drinking, exploring, growing crops, gathering, stealing, trading, doing quests, cooking, sewing, smithing, stealing, raising animals, and perhaps most importantly, decorating. Also, I enjoy stealing stuff.

It looks great and it runs well on my system with highest settings.

All in all, from what I've gathered, this game has come a long way since Early Access. While it may be rough around some edges compared to AAA games, other areas feel very polished and pedantically detailed. It still has even more potential so here's hoping the devs add more buildings and jobs, decorations, quests and whatever else they may come up with. After all, I do need more items to steal.

I just cannot figure out how this game delivers its drug. Is it through keyboard, speakers, leaking through monitor? Weird case.

Anyway, I am not much for grinding, but here, grinding is fun. And for unknown reasons, it perfectly makes sense to just squeeze in one last tree, berry bush or a few fertilizers just before the season ends. There is something strangely addictive going on, something that few games can conjure. 

So excuse me, I must hurry and find a river deep enough to wash myself up, so I can buy that toy sword before the seller falls asleep, allowing me in turn to make my son happier (so he remembers one day when he takes over). Slav background is just a cute bonus. Buy this game and you'll see.

Seja um fazendeiro(a) trabalhe duro. O inverno está chegando.
Case, tenha filhos, trabalhe ainda mais. Sinta orgulho da sua vila crescendo e os aldeões criando suas famílias. Caia nas graças do Rei, ganhe a cresta da sua vila. Veja seus filhos crescerem. Absolut cinema.

I have 200 hours in this game as of the review. I started playing in early access. This game is really fun if you enjoy a hands on colony building and decorating type of situation. I can spend hours just having fun designing the world and it feels very satisfying to see your little village come together. I love building all my little waddle fences and decorating houses. I also love the ambiance of the game. The changing seasons is really fun to watch.

On the downside, the gameplay is VERY slow. It takes a long time to get your town going, and you do a lot of hyper management early on. Gathering sticks, cutting trees, farming, foraging, etc. This is not a game for folk who don't like repetitive gameplay.

The game is presented as being about your "dynasty", but honestly that is the smallest part of the game to me. It takes so long to age up enough to pass the village down to your offspring that you could totally ignore it and build a village within one lifetime and be satisfied. Despite playing 200 hours I've never passed my village down, because it takes SO LONG none of my plays have done it. The relationship aspects to finding a wife are dull to me, and just feels pointlessly grindy. If you play multiplayer and one of you plays male and the other plays female it's way easier because you just accept the relationship and agree to have kids and that's it. 

Also the decorating, while good, has some limitations particularly in the houses and building themselves. There are set decorations you can use in different areas of the house, such as hanging things from the ceiling or having shelves on a wall. But you can't have things like tables and chairs in the house. Something to do with the pathing for the NPCs. So you can't fine tune decorating in that way. You can place lots of things down like cups, bowls, herbs and flowers, etc. The placing system is a little tricky and takes practice. Some people get really good at it and go nuts but it is very time consuming to say... make a basket full of flowers. And if you mess up the physics can make everything go flying. I'd like to see more control over decorating inside the houses, but I don't think that will be happening.

Honestly if you're playing single play you may want to just tweak the game specs to make things move a little faster and a bit easier. For example, tools wear out SO FAST it can feel a little maddening particularly in the early game.

Been looking for a casual game that has base building, city management, and action RPG. As a dad with no time to play, this game is fantastic. You die just respawn. Don't lose all the weapons you have, time you wasted on xp or treasures, etc. Great game.
One of the most addictive, yet kinda boring, games ever. Find myself dying to play it. Can't wait. Get in and stand there.

This game is amazing. It's essentially a medieval life sim that grows from individual survival into nation level management over generations. Your character must have a child to continue, and you inhabit that child when your initial character becomes old. It's a very interesting mechanic. You can also change progression speed. For example, I typically play on 30 day seasons with 10% consumption and growth. This makes it an extremely long, slow game. You can also do the opposite and play on 3 day seasons with 100% consumption/experience which makes it a very fast paced game. There's lots of customization and economic management.

In the future, I'd like to see more co-op options. For example, I wish there was a system where co-op players could respawn at their homes that they build and have permanent characters on game worlds they have loaded into. Maybe even make co-op worlds with multiple players accessible to all players autonomously, so they can continue production without the host?

This is a very slow game, unless you make a mistake and then you are instantly dead. So like real life it's pretty unforgiving. And like real life you will find yourself doing the same repetitive stuff over and over while your tools break. Anyway I guess it's realistic as a simulator but it simulates all the tedium of life.
Love love love the game. Single player, multiplayer, I have gladly sunk many hours into this game.
Love the game, and story so far. My only issue is how many logs it takes to build anything...I've pretty much ran out of trees nearby to continue building.

This game is great, it gives you an amazing prospective of building a village and living in a medieval fantasy,   It also has a great customization which means you have limited time to complete everything or to expand the days in your seasons to be more casual.  With more optimisations to cater to your style of play. Frankly could not recommend a game more.
Really fun game. Late game drops off hard, but that's to be expeected. Something to do with money would be nice. More combat mechanics would be nice.

This game is amazing, hands down. it can be a little grindy though in the beggining. Some times, i would hop on for an hour, but would end up playing for five. Its a great game tho. I totaly recomend. Btw I got it for 17 bucks in medieval fest.

I would say Medieval Dynasty is quite a masterpiece.

I played a lot of survival titles, yet I enjoy this game for a few reasons:

No zombies, no extraterrestrial beings, nor any other supernatural elements in the game so far.

Pure survival mechanics, with the actual goal to create and manage community to generate an income from natural actions - farming, hunting, building, etc

I would sincerely recommend this game to anybody who is looking for a realistic medieval experience.

Thank you very much Render Cube for creating such a gem!

I have close to 100 hours on Xbox (came over to pc when they f'ed with gamepass again a few months ago) and this was one of the first games I purchased after I got a Steam deck. Absolutely incredible gameplay and environment. A game that allows you to slow down and enjoy the simple things in life without ever running out of things to do.

I dont really write reviews alot but this is by far my favorite game i have ever played, its imerseive, its well paced, and they give so much leeway to customizing the world you have, It's great if you like sandbox games and it's story and quests always keep you entertained if you need a break from working on your settlement, my favorite part by far is talking with the NPC's, you can get some pretty funny lines, AND you can play with your friends, boy did that change the game for me, will never forget the time me and my sister were building our first houses in our shared world and i got chased by a bear.
its always been a great game and still is the VR version is hard to get used too but overall its super fun
A nice cozy game. Could be more challenging. Gets boring after a while. ♥♥♥♥ bears, they dont sop chasing you.
Great graphics... I get lost in this game for hours and hours. Definitely recommend.

I thought this game was great, I was building, hunting, gathering, and even doing it with friends!

Then, this one day, a nice new lady came into town. I was excited, letting her move into a house I built with my own hands and showing my love.

Then it happened. This man from the village moved in and stole her from me. So screw your game.

Just kidding, love the game.
Great game, can be hard or easy up to you what settings you like.  This game is a RPG can be played solo or with friends
Love the peace and tranquility and the fun of hunting and discovering bandit camps.
Nice medieval survival/city build & management game. The tutorial isn't the greatest; so, be prepared to either watch some videos on Youtube or play around with the UI to figure things out.
This is an amazing game, recomended to all who love satisfying gameplay
This game is nice but feels like early release. Still many bugs and feel buggy in general.

This game is very good at the beginning, and the references are pretty fun, but it gets kinda boring later on because you don’t really have to do anything. It just becomes micromanaging your villages, and that’s not my thing — but if it is yours, then you won’t mind.
I find this game so cathartic in all the right ways. There's something about taking an hour of real time to just build 1 or 2 buildings. Love this game inside and out.

You want a game that will eat your time and you will say "Thank you"? That is the game for you then. No seriously, it is sort of hardcore but at the same time not really. A lot of grind but some of it is automatable. Listen, if you like some days just want to sit and do some process oriented gameplay - that is it. 

100% worth the money.
pretty good game, almost exactly what you want in a building game

I got this one a while back but have only just got around to having a look. 
I was expecting a bog standard medieval city sim but within a minute or two I realised this is very different. It's a strange little game. I'm going on roughly an hour playing so far but I think I like it.
My character has spawned in a village with a brain injury following a bandit attack and is pretty much pottering around doing his Harvey Price impression attempting to relearn basic tasks. It's currently more Neurodivergence Tycoon than Sim City with lutes and stripey trousers but that's fine. It's not a bad looking title either. I get the feeling I could be sinking more hours into this one. Hopefully my peasant can regain his skills and eventually get revenge on the bandits who turned him into a cabbage.

I'm hooked and over all, it's great. Not all NPC dialogues are voiced and you have to do some reading which takes some getting used to, but over all, it's awesome. I'm getting the relaxing feel and the life building I get when I play Skyrim, and a little pressure that I get when I play Green Hell. This game is the balance I'm looking for between Skyrim and Green Hell.
I luv the game. The little bit that I have played. But even at 1080 and Medium setting the game is choppy and laggy
fun to grind and build. pretty chill and slow paced.

Cozy game! Played once in early access and again recently. Nice game to chill in. Hunting/gathering and building.. eventually you get villagers to do as much or as little of it as you want. Light on combat if that's your thing.

Fantastic settlement-building survival/crafting experience. This is the game that created the genre and no other game comes close! I highly recommend this game if you like open world survival and crafting games, enjoyed the experience of Fallout 4's settlements, and wondered if there was a game 'out there' that focused more on building up a thriving community in a medieval setting. This IS that game! Play it now.

Definitively the best game I've played in a minute, Its a role playing game, survival and settlement builder in one, it not over complicated and is relaxing to spend some time on, with a good soundtrack and graphics.

Before you buy be warned: The game is tediously grindy the more you play it and everything becomes really time consuming. 10 in-game years amount to about 60 hours of gameplay. Your son matures in 18, so do the math. It is supposed to be a long term journey of building your village. If you're into that, good luck and have fun. But if you expect quick, grindless fun then digress.
The game is complex enough for a great experience. But I m too old at this point for this sort of game. I'd rather have a real life wife than a voiceless NPC
so fun you get to explore and you can play with kids by turning of the blood. So awesome

> Be me, unsuspecting player.
> Walk past literally any NPC in town
> "KAAAAAME-HAAAAAAAAAAME-HAAAAAAAAAA"
> Two minutes later, walking past a two year old.
> "I was walking through the forest when I found a little stuck under a log, and I was like "This log has a child"
> Close the game because you can't stop laughing.

This game has the best NPC interactions.

Good as a medieval game. It's not KCD so you can't kill civilians.
Bad point: you get stuck in a tree too often. There is an option to "unstuck" which teleport you to somewhere half a meter away, but if you get stuck when you are chased by bear/wolves/wisent, you simply die from it.
i have become addicted to this game and have more saves/play throughs than id like to admit. excited to dabble in the dlcs soon :"))

Great game, you can do whatever, make a big village, make money, have workers, or you can just have a house and smithy next to a mine. and all the newly added decoration options are quite nice compared to what we had before, now you can place rocks and rock ponds, trees, hedges, among many new fences, gates and plants. it would be nice to see placeable waterfall and ramps which can go up the natural cliffs. sometimes the land angle will really ruin what youre trying to do, that can be annoying especially if you spent hours building and get to the final piece but the land angle wont allow you to place a gateway or pot, or whatever it might be but usually there is a workaround or new idea to close the gap. Its also a bit of a hassle that jobs like water well carrier cant also take the cook role or something, so their not just at the well doing nothing 90% of the time or require micro management.. like if they could just be selected for 3 roles as long as you dont exceede 100% work intensity over all 3, then your wife can do water, wood and food instead of needing micro managing or the player needing atleast 3 villagers just to maintain needs for 1 villager and also they get pregnant and are useless for ages which can really add to the players workload.. its just a game make the pregnant women work, and make the kids pull up the bloody sticks, im out here trying to run a whole village but every season i gotta spent 2 days plucking birch sticks  to expand my maple forest hahahaha, I vote yes to medieval dynasty child workers and pregnant workers

you can get magic potions to heal you, to make you stronger or to instantly become sober. But you can't have two NPCs of the same gender sharing a house. Being gay, somehow that's too unrealistic for a medieval game compared to magic potions.
Poor quality graphics, game freezes during battles, poor starting instructions, over sensitive mouse play.
Its like skryim, except you cant do what you want in the game.
a medieval survival village/town builder with a lot of freedom and pretty indept management options for the village/town. (keeping a wiki page or a video guide open at times is usefull).

[h1]Content & Gameplay: Unfinished & Unbalanced — 5/10[/h1]
Even on default settings, you may reach the end of content before your first child is grown up (max buildings, max product tier). There is no need for heritage or a dynasty; you are already in a repetitive loop with no goals by then. The economic sim and management are poorly made.

[h1]Technically: 10/10[/h1]
Technically very stable and visually beautiful. Performance is great even on aged systems; I found no serious bugs or glitches. (Did not check out the MP feature, but played the related map.)


[h1]Artwork: 6.5/10[/h1]
The OST is too short and becomes boring early. However, disabling music is a bad option because it is the only combat warning. The tunes themselves are ok and not the problem. 3/10
The graphics, and especially the landscapes, are well-made and leave a natural feeling. Much development work went into these maps. Seasonal flavors are also very stunning, including the FX. 9/10

[h1]NPC Villages & Social Sim: Shallow — 4/10[/h1]
The NPCs in the villages may have their daily routine, but it is very basic when it comes to reactions to the story, events, or interaction with the player. I wish they had put more effort here. It might not be functionally necessary, though.

[h1]Quests: 2/10 — Storyline: 8/10[/h1]
The responsible dev team basically failed to deliver something at least rewarding or interesting, and they ignored their own created limiting rules. The storyline is well-written, I would say, and it is not bound to the limitations they introduced to other quests. Whether you like the story or not, some things are well-written and somewhat enjoyable.
The quests themselves are basically delivery missions that make you run back and forth without giving clues about upcoming steps, which makes you run the same routes again even more pointlessly. Most are limited by one season (3 days) and may demand several days without telling you in advance — potentially forcing a failed quest (e.g., "have a beer", nighthunt).

[h1]The Economical Loop: 1/10[/h1]
This is horribly bad design. At some point, you are going to have market stands in your village, and there is a wide variety of products you can make and offer. But all that is pointless because a vendor sells exactly X money per % set into any product based on their diplomacy skill. No matter if they are rubbish, cheap products or high-quality goods, as long as you have enough stock, there is no difference in turnaround. All products have a base price and are being sold when the set percentage of the vendor meets the price of the product.
There is a wide variety of food types, but no actual "demand". Everything will be sold over time just based on the diplomacy skill of the vendor NPC and the % set for the product. There is no specific demand and no difference in the quality of the goods; it is just X money over time. Absolutely boring. So instead of having different products, just have one, sell it, and it is fine.

[h1]Management: 2/10[/h1]
The village management and production management fail to give you the right tools and overview. This is mainly an interface design flaw, but it is also based on avoidable, simplistic bad design.
Workers set up for production will have percentages for different products. But the interface does not show any stock information or production. The workers do not do a different task if they miss some materials for another. You simply set up e.g., 10% logs, 12% sticks, 8% boards, and so on. There should be an option to set a target stock or a production goal. I also miss warnings when the game already knows you are going to run out of stock of a resource. The game does calculate such things, but the management options are lacking here. This leads to tedious micro-management and monitoring of boring management screens, switching back and forth from storage screens and the management of production. They at least should add a button to switch to this screen from inside the management windows. A seasonal report of missing stock with clickable icons (hyperlinks) to reach the management of the buildings would have helped a lot.

[h1]Producing Things — A Waiting Chore: 1/10[/h1]
By default, you craft one good after another while a timer runs, but when you make things in large numbers, there is no discount for mass production. If you cook some meat over a campfire, it might make some sense that you could only cook a very limited amount at a time (the game takes one piece of meat (200 grams) or fish, and then the next; it takes some seconds). But if you have a house with a bigger grill, it is still just one by one. You have a big grill but you put only one piece after another onto it, while there is room for at least 6–10 of them. Same for soup: a big kettle, but you craft just one portion of soup after another. This makes absolutely no sense and is badly balanced, as you eat at least 10–20 pieces of meat per day. There is a menu option to craft an unlimited amount in one loop, which in my opinion is the only way to play this because you won't starve while cooking. Crafting times should scale down for mass production instead of going strictly one-by-one. Baking bread takes 15 seconds, but there is an animation before the timer starts, delaying it even more. I would rather put some bread in the oven, do some other tasks, return after an hour, and get the ready-baked bread instead of sitting there watching a timer count down. Also, drying meat takes seconds instead of putting it on a rack for one or two days.

[h3]Crossbows[/h3]
OK, there are crossbows. It is annoying that the crossbow unloads itself every time you switch to a torch or skin an animal. This mechanic makes it tedious and borderline useless. Having it ready at all times would be unrealistic, true, but something should be done about this to make it more usable.

[h3]Building Hut[/h3]
I really hope for a mod that makes the building hut set up blueprints or at least care for repairs, maintenance, and insulation upgrades automatically. You have to order every single repair. How about ordering the upgrades too?

Pro:
fun
first person
farming, crafting, hunting, building
bandit and bandit camps
NPC actions (fertilizing, plowing, planting, harvesting)

Con:
NPC interactions are text messages (not voiced).
would be cool if they added a roman themed pack with armor, weapons and building parts
Wishing for more crops to plant and other forageables
The devs care about the game. which is rather rare

No matter how hard I try, this game is ABSOLUTELY BORING. I hope the development studio closes down and lays off staff, because nothing about this game is original, or fun. It gives a tutorial about picking up rocks and crafting a knife, but nothing about the intricacies of how to care for your villagers - unless you feel like reading walls of in game text. This is trash. Avoid at all costs.

Almost died from the cold - died from wolfs
watched a friend get yeeted by a bore
great game!
If your looking for a game that constantly crashes... THIS IS THE GAME FOR YOU!!!!

Basically you start as a medieval refugee and have to build your reputation \ assets from scratch. Everything is a slog to grind but the tradeoff is that usually simple items feel rewarding to complete. I did hit a pretty hard stomping point, however, when the third or forth quest I found asked me to get some leather. 

See I tried to kill birds but the spear couldn't hit them. I then tried to capture rabbits, but they didn't give any leather. I finally found a boar and we went blow for blow about three times then I died. I tried throwing a spear at it, barley did any damage. I tried throwing FIVE spears at it, still barley any damage. Eventually I got it to half health and it just took off, ran directly to its group, and they killed me. I died something like 9 more times before I finally managed to chase one down in a different direction. Then i just cheesed the fight by running in a circle and hitting it with an ax. 

And then it didn't drop enough leather to actually finish the quest. 

Right around then I was just like you know what maybe I'm doing this wrong. Maybe there's something in here that I'm missing but genuinely I just didn't want to play it anymore. 

5 out of 10.
I like  the building aspect and free roam to building your  own village.
Features and Updates are behind DLC paywall
Just made it through my 2nd winter (i extended the seasons) and I am really enjoying it so far.
very chill game and a nice game to hang with friends

I have 130 hours on this game, and I still feel like I'm only MAYBE considered midgame on my Co-Op save. It's honestly a blast. To me, these games that have you repeating tasks usually end up feeling like a chore, but Medieval Dynasty somehow doesn't feel that way. It's like a slow-burning build to autonomy and self-sufficiency, but is amazing once tasks you've had to do for a full year are now fully automated. 
Besides all of this, the Dev team is absolutely incredible. They've poured so much love and time into this game, all while listening to the community and feedback. I think I bought this game years ago, playing on and off, and was so excited to see there are STILL major updated coming out and planned DLCs, both free and paid for (which I will happily buy). 
Excited to see what else the Dev team comes up with and would love to see this game be more popular.
This game runs really good with the set up I have. It has creative and good survival settings, very personalizable (is that even a word???). You get exactly what is described. 8/10

I so love this game! I am fond of management games but Medieval Dynasty took it to another level and let players roam around instead of, like any other management game, looking over from above as an invisible hand. I get to experience the events like my people losing firewood or food. If this is a normal management game, I would only read it as text saying your people need food or firewood. Mix it with survival elements? It's perfect for my taste.

A great game that lets you play however you want. Want to focus entirely on hunting and ignore everything else? Go right ahead—just disable hunger, stamina, health loss, and other survival mechanics. Prefer a more challenging experience? Increase taxes, boost the health of bandits and animals, and raise food and water consumption rates.

Thanks to its highly customizable settings, you can tailor the game to your preferred playstyle without opting out of achievements.

The game itself is fun and offers a wide variety of activities, including hunting, gathering, building, trading, crafting, managing villagers, automation, decorating, and much more. There is always something to do, whether you want to focus on expanding your settlement, exploring the world, or simply relaxing and enjoying the scenery.

You can even play with up to three friends in co-op mode. While I haven't tried the multiplayer myself yet, I imagine it adds another enjoyable dimension to the experience.

Performance was also surprisingly good. The game felt a little choppy when I first started playing, but once the shaders had finished loading and compiling, it became buttery smooth. At 1440p with all settings maxed out, I was consistently getting around 160 FPS on my system. My PC specifications are listed below for reference.

Overall, this is a highly enjoyable and versatile game that offers plenty of freedom, content, and replayability.

If I could leave a neutral review, I would. 
The full price simply doesn't reflect what this game has to offer. I bought it at a 50% discount and was fine with that, but in hindsight I wouldn't have paid full price.
I didn't research the development studio beforehand, and I don't particularly care whether it's indie, small, or large. Keep that in mind: I'm reviewing what I expect for my money, nothing more.
The game has a solid foundation, but it's too shallow and unfinished in too many areas.
Combat is rudimentary and borderline pointless. You rarely fight at all. There are wild animals that can be a nuisance early on when you have nothing, and I had two encounters with bandits who were laughably stupid and trivially easy. Armor is equally redundant, since wearing it in summer is barely viable due to a poorly designed temperature tolerance system.
The dynasty system — the game's namesake feature — is genuinely weak. Events are scarce, and the ones that exist are dull. Progression feels artificial prolonged and devoid of any logic. It never feels like an accomplishment. You're essentially just waiting for enough time to pass and enough grinding to happen by you and your NPCs so that your tech points accumulate and you're finally "allowed" to build or craft things that should have been available from the start. There is no research or discovery since every npc village has it available already. It feels like waiting, not like playing.
The building system is overly restrictive, and the maps are too small with few genuinely satisfying locations to build. The world feels lifeless.
On the technical side, performance is acceptable, but the game lacks the polish you'd expect. I encountered very few bugs, and none of them were disruptive enough to break the experience. Visually, it's fine but definitely not up to par for a 2021 release.

With 61 hours played, here's my honest take on who this is for: if you're looking for a simple, low-difficulty medieval village builder and economy sim with light mechanics, something to pass the time with a single playthrough and no high expectations you'll probably get something out of it. But if you're looking for anything beyond that surface level, you'll feel the absence very quickly.
It feels as though the developers reached a point where more depth, more immersion, and more meaningful systems were clearly within reach and then stopped. Instead of building further, they appear to have repackaged the same foundation with a different (japanese) aesthetic and released it as a new title.
A little light on mechanics and feels unfinished in some areas, whilst initially fun, progress is glacial.

As someone who played Sengoku Dynasty first, this game is far better! I personally love tedious grinding and this game is perfect for kicking up on the couch an relaxing. There's also enough action like hunting and combat to keep it interesting. I have yet to play as my heir but looking forward to building my dynasty.
Love this game. Hours of fun and replayability. The devs keep coming out with free updates to make quaility of life better, which is awesome. Plus the storylines are good too.
Great settlement building, interesting and engaging main and side quests, and lots of fun easter eggs for gamers!
This game is addictive for people who love chill games and building villages. I can't wait to see what else they add as each development keeps me coming back and losing a whole weekend to it.

Medieval Dynasty is honestly boring and extremely clunky, especially when playing co-op. I played it with my girlfriend, and we both felt like the progression was painfully slow and not very rewarding.

Almost everything in the game feels stiff and outdated. The management systems, building, gathering resources, questing, and even basic gameplay all feel rough and awkward. The quests are especially annoying because they constantly send you from one town to another, and everything is so far apart that it becomes more frustrating than fun.

The combat is also terrible. Wolves just rush at you and bite you, and the whole system feels clunky as hell. At one point, I found some enemies in the northeast, started fighting them, then somehow got teleported right between them and died almost instantly. It felt broken and unfair.

I also expected the NPCs and my village to feel more alive, but they really do not. For example, I thought villagers would actually go out and collect wood, but it seems like they just generate resources instead of physically doing the work.

Overall, Medieval Dynasty feels very wooden, slow, boring, and frustrating. The idea is interesting, but the execution feels too rough and outdated for me to enjoy.
